title: Container Threat Models, Users, Capabilities, Seccomp, Rootless, and Daemon Authority · Delivery Case: Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel
summary: A third-party document converter handles hostile files but requests root, privileged mode, every device, and the Docker socket. Planned artifact: Add and verify the docker threat boundary host kernel increment in least-privilege container threat model and hardening profile. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default.
Use config, profiles, images, models, variables, consistency checks, dry-run, and changed-file review before mutating a stack.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# docker-basics cumulative container delivery portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never connect to a Docker daemon, mount a socket, or execute learner commands.
workspace: docker
version-baseline:
  engine: 29.6.1
  compose: 5.3.1
  buildx: 0.35.0
  buildkit: 0.31.1
  dockerfile-frontend: 1.25
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-environment
evidence:

steps.title: Predict Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel · Theory Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory document parser runs privileged as root, mounts daemon authority, writes the image filesystem, has network access, and no resource caps. Remove authority and state the remaining shared-kernel risk. Competency probe: Model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel contract for the supplied data.

content.title: Reasoning move

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example document parser runs privileged as root, mounts daemon authority, writes the image filesystem, has network access, and no resource caps. Remove authority and state the remaining shared-kernel risk. Competency probe: Model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default. A defensible solution applies Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to least-privilege container threat model and hardening profile.

content.code:
# Evidence: docker-worked-docker-security-least-privilege-5
  - competency: docker-worked-docker-security-least-privilege-5
    artifact: security-policy.yaml
    decision: worked docker security least privilege 5
    scenario-variant: 4
    environment: ephemeral development host with constrained disk and memory
    fault-injection: rebuild after the approved base digest is withdrawn
    retained-proof: accessible recovery runbook with rollback rehearsal timing
    case-key: worked5
    change: |
      user: 10001:10001
      read_only: true
      cap_drop: [ALL]
      security_opt: [no-new-privileges:true]
      tmpfs: [/tmp]
      network_mode: none
    verify: docker inspect document-worker --format json
    failure: Reject privileged mode, daemon-socket mounting, broad devices, root-by-default, or a claim that containers form a hostile-code sandbox.
    repair: Remove daemon authority, choose rootless or user namespaces where applicable, drop capabilities, bound writable paths, and state residual kernel risk.
content.caption: Runnable Docker artifact and transfer-evidence contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: Any process inside a container is safe to run because namespaces prevent host impact. This fails the Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.

content.label: Research anchor
content.value: Docker Engine Security, Rootless, Seccomp, and API Protection (Docker Engine 29 security guidance, current 2026-07-14) — Controls daemon authority, rootless and user namespaces, users, capabilities, seccomp, mandatory access control, secrets, privileged boundaries, and socket protection.

content.label: Mastery evidence
content.value: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default.
